- [WA] Prime-field combinatorics under composite moduli assumes every nonzero denominator is invertible. A strong adversarial shape keeps Pascal or multiplicative branches for tiny `n`, tiny `k`, and detected prime moduli, then reuses `fac/ifac`, Fermat inverses, or field generating-function algebra for the broad composite case. It survives prime-looking weak tests, but zero divisors, prime powers, and factorial valuations can erase terms before the formula expects them to.
- [WA] Modular exact-division shortcuts assume integer cancellation and modular cancellation are interchangeable. The adversarial solution normalizes residues, cancels a visible global `gcd`, handles the `gcd(den, M) = 1` bucket correctly, and guesses one representative for the noninvertible middle. It fails when the local prime-power constraints imply no quotient, multiple quotients, or valuation-sensitive cancellation.
- [WA] Batch inverse prefix-product template assumes every denominator in the batch is a unit. A strong adversarial version separates obvious zeros, handles prime moduli and `gcd(x, M) = 1` entries exactly, then reuses one product inverse or prefix/suffix inverse pass for the rest. Composite moduli with one nonunit factor can invalidate many quotients at once, and the first locally skipped denominator may change the whole reconstructed expression.
- [WA] Expectation, probability, or recurrence solving over `mod M` treats the system as a field. A plausible solver handles tiny states exactly, special-cases prime moduli, and then runs modular Gaussian elimination or recurrence division with a best-effort pivot policy. Composite moduli break it through zero-divisor determinants, absorbing classes with noninvertible denominators, or rational values that must be reduced before taking residues.
- [WA] Euler/Fermat exponent reduction is used as if the base is always a unit modulo `M`. The heuristic handles `a = 0,1`, small tower heights, and coprime cases, then reduces all remaining exponents modulo `phi(M)` or `lambda(M)`. Nonunits, high prime powers, and real preperiods before the multiplicative cycle change the answer while random coprime data looks fine.
- [WA] Orders, primitive roots, and discrete logs assume the unit group is cyclic and its order is fully factored. The adversarial solver verifies small prime factors, supports prime moduli exactly, and runs baby-step giant-step on the claimed subgroup. Composite noncyclic groups, missing large order factors, and subgroup membership constraints produce locally plausible but globally false residues.
- [TLE] Sequence period detection budgets only for random short cycles. The adversarial route simulates until a repeat in a projected residue state, then jumps by the apparent period. Weak data with small moduli passes, while long preperiods, hidden state components, or lcm-composed periods force near-linear simulation in the true period.
- [WA] CRT merging only partially checks non-coprime consistency. A nontrivial wrong merge normalizes residues, uses `gcd` in the formula, sorts equations, and rechecks recent constraints after each merge. Incompatible repeated prime-power constraints or a later equation whose modulus divides an earlier one with a different residue expose the missing global consistency proof.
- [WA] Garner or coprime-residue CRT transplant assumes the residue-vector template still works after moduli are merely normalized, sorted, or split by visible gcds. It can fall back to extended CRT for two equations and handle fully coprime batches, but mixed prime-power chains need a compatible solution modulo the lcm rather than independent digits modulo the product.
- [MLE] CRT, lcm, or period aggregation materializes by the combined modulus. The implementation may cap answers numerically, but still allocates transition tables, visited arrays, residue buckets, or period vectors of size `lcm(m_i)` or the product of coprime factors. Weak tests with aligned moduli fit; adversarial coprime chains make the mathematical state space far larger than the input size.
- [RE] CRT/lcm range blowups are treated as harmless once the final answer is capped. The solver forms the combined modulus or step before proving it is needed, then uses that value as a divisor, loop stride, array extent, or normalization base. Small products behave, but legal chains of near-limit coprime moduli can create invalid intermediate ranges before any "too large" verdict is returned.
- [WA] Lucas-style digit decomposition is applied outside its theorem regime. The adversarial solution is exact for prime `p`, falls back to Pascal for small `n`, and for composite moduli either applies Lucas per base-`M` digit or combines prime-mod answers without prime-power lifting terms. Prime powers and composite moduli require carries, valuations, and CRT reconstruction the shortcut never models.
- [WA] Prime-power valuation collapse tracks only whether `p` divides a term, not the exact depth. The heuristic keeps residues exact and maybe handles `v_2` or small exponents up to a cap, then treats all larger positive valuations identically in factorials, binomials, divisor formulas, or congruence compatibility. One extra power of the same prime can change cancellation, zero tests, and local counts.
- [WA] Squarefree-kernel compression assumes the support of prime factors is enough. The adversarial solver factors enough to know which primes appear, memoizes kernels, and uses squarefree formulas for Mobius signs, coprimality DP, or inclusion-exclusion. Repeated prime factors alter divisor counts, totients, Mobius zeroing, and local multiplicities.
- [TLE] Worst-case factorization is priced from average smoothness instead of the input ceiling. The implementation has fast paths for even numbers, small primes, repeated-query memoization, and probable-prime exits, so weak random data looks fine. Many distinct large primes, near-limit semiprimes, or query sets chosen to defeat memoization make arithmetic preprocessing dominate the run.
- [TLE] Factorization fallback work scales with the number of hard tails, not just the largest value. A solver that is fine for one large semiprime may still retry or trial-divide each leftover independently across `q` queries, divisor DP states, or moduli. Weak tests contain one hard number; official-style batches make the factor oracle the bottleneck.
- [WA] Partial factorization treats every large leftover as one prime. The solver trial-divides by small primes, handles small values and obvious prime powers exactly, and then feeds the remaining tail into divisor, totient, Mobius, or order formulas as exponent one. Balanced semiprimes and products of two large factors beyond the trial bound poison formulas depending on the real split.
- [MLE] Sieve-based preprocessing assumes the maximum value is the right scale. A strong adversarial version uses linear sieve arrays, compressed types, segmented chunks for some branches, and early exits for lower subtasks, then still stores SPF, Mobius, divisor heads, prefix DP, or per-value query state up to the full maximum. Sparse inputs with a huge coordinate ceiling make memory fail before arithmetic begins.
- [MLE] Divisor-list materialization stores all divisors because each individual value looks manageable. The adversarial solution precomputes `divs[x]` for every `x`, caches full divisor vectors per query, or stores transitions for every divisor pair. Highly composite values, many related multiples, or a dense interval near the maximum push total materialized divisor count past the intended input-output scale.
- [TLE] Divisor explosion is hidden behind a smoothness assumption. The solver enumerates all divisors, all squarefree subsets, or all factor-exponent combinations and then prunes by the target predicate. Random values have few divisors, but highly composite numbers and dense divisor intervals make the enumeration itself the hard instance.
- [WA] Multiplicative-function formulas are computed from capped exponents or a factor sketch. The route handles primes, squarefree values, and small prime powers exactly, then clamps every exponent above two or merges an unfactored tail into one local factor. Repeated powers make `phi`, `mu`, `sigma`, divisor counts, and local DP weights diverge sharply.
- [TLE] Congruence or Diophantine constraints are solved by pseudo-polynomial residue scans. The adversarial route reduces by `gcd`, chooses the smaller modulus or coefficient range to iterate, and adds hashing or meet-in-the-middle for weak limits. Coprime moduli near the ceiling, sparse valid residues, or many independent congruences force a scan proportional to the modulus rather than the input length.
- [TLE] Big-integer or exact-rational arithmetic is treated as a constant-factor safety net. The solver lifts modular formulas to exact fractions, arbitrary-precision products, determinant numerators, or unreduced lcms before reducing at the end. Weak tests keep values small; large binomial layers, CRT products, rational recurrences, or repeated normalization make bit length the real complexity.
- [RE] Recursive number-theory decomposition assumes the component count is subtask-sized. Linear recursive CRT merges, recursive factor-component DFS, Stern-Brocot or continued-fraction descent, and divisor generation by one frame per prime/exponent all pass small examples. When the input model allows hundreds of thousands of moduli, factors given explicitly, or long quotient chains, the call depth itself becomes the failure mode.
- [WA] Modular convolution or NTT code assumes the modulus has the needed root structure. The heuristic handles known friendly primes, uses CRT through a fixed set of transform primes for some outputs, and falls back to naive multiplication for tiny sizes. It breaks when the requested modulus lacks a suitable primitive root, coefficient growth exceeds the reconstruction range, or the transform length needs an unsupported power.
- [WA] Integer reconstruction from friendly primes assumes the true value is below their product. The adversarial solution computes exact answers modulo common NTT or hash primes and maps back to a signed representative if it looks reasonable. Adversarial instances with a larger exact count, negative-representative ambiguity, or modulus-dependent cancellation need more primes or a real bound proof.
- [WA] Rational reconstruction assumes the intended numerator and denominator are small and unique. The solver computes residues modulo one or more primes, runs continued fractions or bounded search, and accepts the first fraction passing sampled checks. Multiple fractions can share the used residue product, denominators may not be coprime to a modulus, and the true bounds can exceed the reconstruction proof.
- [TLE] Divisibility inclusion-exclusion scales with dense prime interaction, not just the number of distinct primes. The heuristic removes duplicates, compresses by squarefree masks, handles small masks exactly, and truncates rare large-prime interactions. Sparse random divisibility graphs pass, but dense shared-prime constructions or repeated prime powers produce a state space too large to enumerate.
